intro: Sihor (also spelled Shihor) was a waterway on the border of Egypt, used to designate the southwestern extent of Canaan or the Nile itself. In Joshua 13:3 it defines the southern limit of unconquered Canaanite territory: from Sihor, which is east of Egypt, as far as the border of Ekron in the north. Isaiah 23:3 describes Sihor's harvest as Tyre's revenue, linking it to the Egyptian grain trade. Jeremiah 2:18 asks Israel why they go to Egypt to drink from the waters of Sihor. The name is generally understood as a variant of Shichor meaning black or turbid, often identified with the easternmost branch of the Nile or with the Wadi el-Arish, the traditional boundary between Egypt and Canaan.
